turns.content: I got my members compiling after adding the root Cargo.toml with workspace.members, but I'm manually repeating edition and authors in every package. Is there a way to let them inherit from the root instead?

You can define version and authors once in the root Cargo.toml under workspace.package and then omit those fields in each member crate. Rust will automatically inherit them because missing fields default to the values defined at the workspace level, so you only need a single source of truth for metadata.
That sounds convenient but what happens if I add a new package later? Do I still have to edit every existing file or just one place?

You only edit the root Cargo.toml workspace.package section. Any new crate you add will automatically pick up those values without needing any changes in its own config, which prevents drift and keeps your build consistent across all crates.

I'm trying to port our monorepo from Go modules, where I manually synced version and author fields across all sub-projects. In Cargo, does the workspace.json define a single source of truth that automatically propagates those values to every member crate like the Go go.mod inheritance model?

Exactly, you are correct about the single source of truth concept. The workspace.json acts as the central configuration file where you define the version, authors, and edition once. When you add a new member crate to this list using the package field, those inherited values automatically apply without needing to redefine them in each individual Cargo.toml file.
That sounds like what I need for consistency, but does this inheritance mechanism only affect metadata or does it also enforce trait bounds and type coherence across the workspace members?

The inheritance is strictly about configuration metadata propagation; it does not enforce trait bounds or type coherence. The actual type safety and trait implementations are handled by the compiler when each member crate is built individually, even though they share the same edition and author settings from workspace.json.

I just shipped a fix for a memory leak in our main application crate using Box and Rc, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to manage ownership here. I've been told that the standard library types like Arc and RefCell might help with this. Should I refactor my code to use those instead of Box and Rc?

For managing shared ownership across threads in Rust, you should definitely consider using Arc along with RefCell or Mutex depending on whether your data is mutable or immutable. While Box can be used for heap allocation, it does not provide the same thread safety guarantees that Arc does when combined with interior mutability traits like RefCell. If your code needs to share state between threads without explicit locking, switching to Arc<RefCell<T>> or Arc<Mutex<T>> would align better with idiomatic Rust practices and prevent data races.

Wait, so I should just replace my Box and Rc usage? That sounds like a lot of refactoring. But what about the versioning across our monorepo? We have multiple crates in our workspace and I need to ensure everyone uses the same edition and author settings. Is there a way to share that metadata without duplicating it everywhere?

You don't necessarily replace everything at once, but for new code paths involving shared state across threads, Arc combined with interior mutability is the preferred pattern over raw pointers or Box alone. Regarding your workspace configuration, yes, Cargo supports inheriting version and edition settings from the root workspace file called Cargo.toml in the workspace directory. You can define version, authors, and edition once there, and then each package member will automatically inherit these values unless explicitly overridden.

Actually, I tried adding those fields to my individual crate's Cargo.toml and it didn't pick them up from the root. It seems like inheritance isn't working as expected for our setup. Can you explain more about how exactly workspace.package inheritance works? Why did my manual configuration fail?

The issue is that edition, authors, and version are not stored in individual package Cargo.toml files; they are defined only once under [workspace] in the root Cargo.toml. When you manually specify those fields inside a child crate's Cargo.toml, your code effectively overrides the inherited values instead of relying on them. To fix this, remove the redundant edition, authors, and version declarations from each package's Cargo.toml so that the workspace-level configuration becomes the single source of truth for all members in your workspace.
